coredata.dc:title: A redox-coupled carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheets based sensory platform for label-free and sensitive detection of E. coli

coredata.dc:description: An enzymatic redox reaction was exploited in developing a novel carbon dots (CDs)-manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets based fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive, selective, and rapid detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli) in aqueous media. The detection of E. coli is achieved by rationally placing a relay of redox reactions mediated by enzyme and a redox couple of p-benzoquinone (BQ) and hydroquinone (HQ). Initially, CDs-MnO2 forms a FRET conjugate and the inherent fluorescence of CDs remains in the turn-off state. Presumably, the enzymes present in E. coli respiratory pathway (such as NADH-quinone reductase), converts BQ to HQ by two-electron reduction, which in turn reacts with MnO2 nanosheets to reduce it to free Mn2+ ions. As MnO2 nanosheets disintegrate, the CDs are released from electrostatic interaction and go to a disperse phase in the aqueous solution to emit blue fluorescence. Thus, it devises a label-free sensing assay for the detection of live E. coli. The LOD was determined as 50 CFU/mL for E. coli. The utility of this cost-effective sensing tool was demonstrated in real-sample analysis and quantitation of E. coli was done in rainwater and food samples. Further, the fluorescent images of bacterial solutions and agar-polymer based test-strips were captured on a smartphone and analyzed using ImageJ for a practical demonstration of on-site detection of E. coli. The present method with merits like cost-effective synthesis from biomass, rapid detection, low detection limit, high selectivity offers a viable alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of E. coli.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An enzymatic redox reaction was exploited in developing a novel carbon dots (CDs)-manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets based fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive, selective, and rapid detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli) in aqueous media. The detection of E. coli is achieved by rationally placing a relay of redox reactions mediated by enzyme and a redox couple of p-benzoquinone (BQ) and hydroquinone (HQ). Initially, CDs-MnO2 forms a FRET conjugate and the inherent fluorescence of CDs remains in the turn-off state. Presumably, the enzymes present in E. coli respiratory pathway (such as NADH-quinone reductase), converts BQ to HQ by two-electron reduction, which in turn reacts with MnO2 nanosheets to reduce it to free Mn2+ ions. As MnO2 nanosheets disintegrate, the CDs are released from electrostatic interaction and go to a disperse phase in the aqueous solution to emit blue fluorescence. Thus, it devises a label-free sensing assay for the detection of live E. coli. The LOD was determined as 50 CFU/mL for E. coli. The utility of this cost-effective sensing tool was demonstrated in real-sample analysis and quantitation of E. coli was done in rainwater and food samples. Further, the fluorescent images of bacterial solutions and agar-polymer based test-strips were captured on a smartphone and analyzed using ImageJ for a practical demonstration of on-site detection of E. coli. The present method with merits like cost-effective synthesis from biomass, rapid detection, low detection limit, high selectivity offers a viable alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:para: For years, major health threats to humans are caused by foodborne diseases [1]. Pathogenic bacteria are the root cause of food and water contamination, posing substantial financial burdens on public healthcare [2,3]. Among numerous bacterial pathogens, E. coli is the most common and frequently causes serious health issues like urinary tract infections, kidney failure, meningitis, anaemia, and haemorrhagic diarrhea [4,5]. Therefore, rapid and sensitive detection, as well as discrimination of this bacteria, is essential for environmental science, food safety, clinical diagnosis, and treatment [6,7]. To detect and contain bacterial contamination and spread of the infection, many traditional laboratory methods are employed like, standard plate count (SPC) [8] and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [9,10]. Although these classic bacterial detection methods are reliable and accurate, the time-consuming and sensitive operations, the requirement of trained personnel limit their frequent and broad applications [11]. To overcome the drawbacks of traditional methods substantial attention has been focused on developing strategies for rapid and specific bacterial detection with techniques like fluorimetry [12–16], colorimetric detection [17–19], electrochemical methods [19–21], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [22], mass spectrometry [23], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [24], electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [25] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) [26]. Among them, ELISA based methods are rapid, sensitive and selective due to the immobilization of recognition units on a solid surface which is highly specific for the targeted analyte. However, fancy labeling with bio-entities makes them very expensive and time-consuming. We presumed, label-free detection tools as less-expensive and a rational design can make them highly selective and sensitive as well.

Over the years, fluorimetry has received significant focus as one of the better methods for imaging, monitoring and diagnosis purposes [27,28]. Recently, there is a paradigm shift from conventional organic dyes to fluorescent nanomaterials [29]. In this direction, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained popularity, owing to their unparalleled advantages like green synthesis from biomass, water-solubility, steady fluorescence with good quantum yield, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [30,31]. These exceptional properties grant looming applications in the field of catalysis [32], sensing [33], bio-imaging [34], and so on [35]. On the other hand, MnO2 nanosheets, a 2D material with an exceptionally broad absorption band usually employed as fluorescence quencher for organic dyes and fluorescent nanomaterials [36–38]. Interestingly, the right combination of donor and acceptor molecules like CDs and MnO2 nanosheets has been used as a FRET-based sensing platform for biologically relevant reducing species like GSH [38,39], ascorbic acid (AA) [40]. Related to this work, the use of benzoquinone (BQ) in sensing application in determining toxicity, glucose levels, and substrate-oxidizing activity in microbial metabolism is well-documented [19,41]. The independent use of functional MnO2 nanosheets in sensing applications have also been documented [36]. We envisaged the use of BQ and MnO2 in a relay process for selective and quantitative detection of E. coli.

There are few reports on the use of carbon dots (CDs) for the detection and imaging of E. coli [42–48]. Wang et al. adopted a simple strategy for E. coli only at higher concentrations (105 CFU/mL) by electrostatic interaction in a non-specific manner [42]. Nandi et al. grafted amphiphilic units on carbon dots for bacterial detection and imaging [43]. Bhaisare et al. developed magnetic carbon dots using magnetic nanoparticles and chitosan for the pathogen detection [44]. Chandra et al. used an antibiotic, amikacin embedded fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection of E. coli [45]. Strategically, these reported functionalized CDs suffer from one or more demerits like multi-step surface modification, complex procedure, poor selectivity, poor detection limit, inadequate stability, etc [49] and they are unable to differentiate between live and dead bacteria. Hence, there remain substantial opportunities for the development of label-free, simple and cost-effective sensing systems for E. coli detection, particularly the live cells.

Herein, we present the use of an enzyme-mediated redox relay reaction in CDs-MnO2 FRET-based sensing assay for label-free detection of E. coli. It was envisaged that during cell respiration of E. coli, redox enzymes such as NADH-quinone reductase directly convert BQ to HQ which in turn reacts with MnO2 nanosheets to quickly reduce it to free Mn2+ ions. As MnO2 nanosheets disintegrate, the CDs are released back into the aqueous media resulting in a turn-on fluorescence response. Our fluorescence assay can specifically detect and distinguish live E. coli from other bacterial types (Gram-positive, Gram-negative, acid-fast).

Raw potato (Solanum tuberosum) was purchased from Vasco local market, Goa, India. Frequently used chemicals like potassium permanganate (KMnO4), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and MES buffer (morpholinoethane sulphonic acid monohydrate, 99 %) were procured from LOBA Chemie, Pvt. Ltd., India. Luria Broth was purchased from Hi-Media Laboratories, Mumbai. All other chemicals and solvents used were of AR grade, obtained from SD Fine-Chem Limited, Mumbai, India or Merck, India and were used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using Millipore water (18 MΩ-cm @ 25 °C).

All the fluorescence measurements were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission spectra in the range of 366–650 nm on a JASCO FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (JASCO Corp., Japan); the slit width was 2.5 nm for both excitation and emission. UV–vis spectra were taken on a JASCO V-550; the bandwidth and data pitch were set as 1 nm. IR Affinity-1 FTIR Spectrophotometer, Shimadzu was used to record IR spectra. Surface morphologies were recorded on atomic force microscope, NT-MDT make. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were recorded on a Quanta 250 FEG (FEI make). High-resolution transmission electron microscope images were recorded on a field emission gun-transmission electron microscope 120/200 kV (HR-TEM 120/200 kV, make: JEOL, model: JEM 2100 F) with operating voltages: 120−200 kV, Resolution: Point: 0.19 nm Line: 0.1 nm). The time-resolved fluorescence measurements were carried out using a time-correlated-single-photon-counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH, UK). Zeta potential and DLS were measured using NanoPlus with NanoPlus AT. Optical micrographs were taken on an inverted microscope, IX-51 (Olympus) with fluorescence attachment. ImageJ public domain image processing software was used for solution phase detection.

E. coli, S. aureus, M. smegmatis, E. faecalis, V. cholerae and P. aeruginosa were selected for the experiments. The cultures were routinely grown overnight (incubated for 12 h, at 37 °C) and sustained in Luria Bertani broth for E. coli, Tryptic Soy Broth (Becton-Dickinsonand Company, USA) for S. aureus, and Middlebrook 7H9 broth base(Hi-Media, India) for M. smegmatis, respectively. For routine culture maintenance, agar was added as per the requirement. The OD of the bacterial cultures at OD600 ∼0.5, was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (for details see supporting information (SI)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized following previously reported procedures (for details see SI) [33].

The sensing probe, CDs-MnO2 conjugate was prepared by mixing a solution of CDs with aqueous KMnO4 in MES buffer [33]. Briefly, a stock solution of CDs (100 μL), MES buffer (0.5 M, pH 6, 100 μL), KMnO4 (0.1 M, 50 μL) were mixed in 750 μL of ultrapure water for preparation of every 1 mL of sensing solution. The solution was sonicated at room temperature for 30 min to obtain a brown solution. The above solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm, and the resulting precipitate was collected. It was washed three times with Millipore water to remove excess KMnO4 and re-suspended in 1 mL of ultrapure water by sonication for 5 min to get the stock solution of carbon dots on MnO2 nanosheets.

1 mL of different concentrations of bacterial solution (1.0 × 102 to 2.0 × 109 CFU/mL) were prepared using isotonic solution (0.9 % NaCl). A stock solution of BQ (0.4 M) was prepared (also in 0.9 % NaCl), 10 μL of the solution was added to previously diluted samples of bacteria and mixed well before incubating for 1 h at room temperature. Further, 1 mL of sensing solution was added into these series of bacterial samples to record fluorescence response. In order to investigate the selectivity of the sensing assembly towards E. coli, few Gram-positive strains like S. aureus and E. faecalis, Gram-negative strains of bacteria like P. aeruginosa and V. cholerae and acid-fast M. smegmatis were used. For tolerance study of redox species, ascorbic acid (AA) and GSH stock solution (1 mM) were prepared in Millipore water. Different concentrations of AA and GSH were spiked in 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL of E. coli and incubated for 1 h. This series of solutions was further incubated in BQ to record fluorescence response against sensing assay. For samples like potato pulp and apple juice, the pulps and roughages were filtered and the extracts were used for analysis. The real samples were directly spiked with a known concentration of E. coli to make the final concentrations 1.0 × 102, 1.0 × 103, 1.0 × 104 and 1.0 × 105 CFU/mL, and after incubated for 1 h, the corresponding fluorescence response was recorded. The presence of live bacteria in real samples was viewed under an Olympus IX51 inverted microscope, and both phase-contrast and fluorescence images were captured. All experiments were carried out at room temperature (25 °C), performed in triplicate, and the average data were reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for dead cell response towards sensing assay
ce:section.ce:para: The routinely grown E. coli colonies were extracted and suspended in an isotonic solution, and incubated without adding appropriate nutrients. The aliquots of bacterial samples were taken out at different time intervals (0, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h), assayed using the standard protocol and fluorescence output was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for image analysis and solid phase detection of E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: As described above, the solutions used for the fluorescence study were directly subjected to image analysis by ImageJ software [50]. For, solid-phase detection, CDs-MnO2 embedded agar-polymer films were prepared by adding agar in CDs-MnO2 aqueous solution, then microwaved and dried at room temperature. It was cut into small pieces and those were dipped into the aqueous solution of BQ containing different concentrations of E. coli (1.0 × 102, 1.0 × 104 and 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL) for 2 h, and allowed to dry. The fluorescence images were taken under long UV light source (365 nm). All the images were taken by Samsung M31 smartphone with 64 megapixels camera without any filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the CDs-MnO2 conjugate was prepared by simple mixing of carbon dots with aqueous KMnO4 in MES buffer and sonicating the resulting solution for 30 min at room temperature [33]. The absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets displayed a blue shift in the absorption band (Fig. S4a, SI) indicating an adduct/conjugate formation between the CDs and the in situ formed MnO2 nanosheets. Alternatively, the CDs solution was mixed with the MnO2 nanosheets prepared separately by reducing the KMnO4 in the MES buffer solution [33]. Both these methods provided blue-shifted absorption band and the strong absorption band of CDs at 310 nm disappeared in agreement with the reported formation of CDs dispersed MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S4a, SI). The CDs-MnO2 conjugate displayed a broad absorption region having good overlap with the emission band of the CDs, allowing them to interact through an energy transfer process within their proximity in the conjugate [51]. The formation of CDs-MnO2 conjugate was also documented from the fluorescence measurements of CDs in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets. The fluorescence response of CDs and the CDs-MnO2 conjugate revealed severe quenching of the emission intensity to a bare minimum level, pointing to an efficient energy transfer interaction among the excited states of CDs and the MnO2 nanosheets and is attributed due to the feasibility of a FRET mechanism operating in the conjugate [33,51]. In addition, MnO2 nanosheets possess a high specific surface area and excellent loading capability. Presumably, the difference in the surface charge (for MnO2 nanosheets the surface charge is −17.25 mV) and its wrinkled structure provides enough two-dimensional space to accommodate the smaller sized CDs to form a close conjugation of CDs-MnO2 nanosheets by the electrostatic interaction with the positively charged pockets, particularly in close proximity to the metal centers. Thus, the surface charge of the conjugate slightly increases to -18.04 mV (Fig. S1, SI). Besides, it is reasonable to assume that the carboxylate groups of CDs engage in the partial overlap with the empty orbitals of Mn4+ centers allowing adherence of CDs on the MnO2 nanosheets [52]. At close proximity, Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and MnO2 nanosheets ensures that the CDs remain at fluorescence quenched state as long as they adhere onto MnO2 nanosheets. In this regard, we have further explored the emission results by time resolved fluorescence measurements using a TCSPC experimental setup. As shown in Fig. S4b of SI, the fluorescence decay trace of CDs alone, monitored at 450 nm, displayed a mutiexponential decay profile with an average lifetime of ∼ 4.56 ns and is in good agreement with the excited state lifetime of CDs reported from other origins [38]. However, with the addition of MnO2 nanosheets, the excited state decay of CDs became faster and in the presence of MnO2 the average lifetime reduced to ∼ 3.49 ns (Table S2, SI) in accordance to the generally discussed FRET as the quenching mechanism in the CDs-MnO2 conjugate.

Further, the electron microscopic images showed the morphologies of MnO2 nanosheets and CDs-MnO2 conjugate (Fig. 3 ). In HRTEM, it was seen that several large two-dimensional MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 3a) are spread in the area with apparent adherence of CDs on them (Fig. 3c). Similarly, FESEM images showed some roughness on the 2D surface of MnO2 nanosheets after modification with CDs (Fig. 3b and d). As expected, the particle size analysis also revealed that the MnO2 nanosheets (211 nm) retain their structural integrity (224 nm) even after CDs are adhered on them (Fig. S3).

At the outset, the concentration of p-benzoquinone (BQ), required for the preconceived enzymatic redox reaction, was identified. The BQ concentrations were varied (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 mM) against two different concentrations of E. coli (1.0 × 109 and 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL) and the corresponding fluorescence outputs were obtained from the CDs-MnO2 conjugate, present in the aqueous assay. After incubating above series of mixtures for 1 h, fluorimetric responses were recorded (Fig. S5a and b). The intensities of these solutions were enhanced with increasing concentrations of BQ till 4 mM and then gradually decreased (Fig. S5c). Presumably, up to a certain concentration of BQ, the ideal concentration of hydroquinone (HQ) is produced by enzymatic redox reaction, which in turn reduces MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+; however, with the presence of large excess of BQ, a simultaneous BQ-HQ complex formation [19] is more prevalent resulting in a gradual decrease in fluorescence output from the solution. It is also indicated by the color intensification of the red-colored BQ-HQ complex (Fig. S6). Therefore, 4 mM was chosen as the optimal BQ concentration for E. coli detection. In a separate study, the concentrations of KMnO4 to obtain the highest fluorescence quenching of a fixed volume of CDs was optimized as 4 mM (Fig. S7 of SI). About 90 % of quenching of the inherent fluorescence of CDs was achieved at this concentration of KMnO4, suggesting the availability of sufficient numbers of MnO2 nanosheets to impart fluorescence quenching by electrostatic interaction with CDs. As a control experiment, the CDs solution was allowed to interact with different concentrations of E. coli (5.0 × 101 to 5.0 × 108 CFU/mL) followed by the addition of BQ solution and in the absence of MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S8, SI). No fluorescence quenching and subsequent turn-on response was observed; the inherent fluorescence of CDs was unperturbed. This indicates the role of MnO2 nanosheets in this redox relay process and also signifies no interference of E. coli cell surface on CDs.

The analytical utility of this fluorescent sensing assay was demonstrated by varying the concentration of E. coli under optimized aqueous assay condition. Thus, E. coli (2.0 × 109 CFU/mL) culture was first diluted to required concentrations, BQ in isotonic solution (4 mM) was added and incubated for 1 h. To it, the solution of CDs-MnO2 conjugate was added and fluorimetric outputs from the solutions were recorded immediately. The non-virulent strain, DH5α was used for all the fluorimetric studies considering it possesses similar characteristics of pathogenic strain O157:H7. As shown in Fig. 4 , a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity was observed from the aqueous solution of CDs-MnO2 conjugate, on the incremental addition of E. coli cells in the sensing assay (Fig. 4a). The intensity of the characteristic fluorescence peak of CDs at λmax 445 nm was enhanced up to 16-folds with the successive addition of E. coli colonies and reached saturation level at 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL of E. coli. The extent of release of CDs to the solution phase is directly dependent on the concentration of HQ diffused from the E. coli cells into the solution, and the corresponding fluorescence output holds a linear relationship from 5.0 × 101 to 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL of E. coli with high regression coefficient (R2 = 0.99699) (Fig. 4b). This sensing assay allows the detection of E. coli as low as 50 CFU/mL, making CDs-MnO2 conjugate a better sensory platform for testing environmental or clinical samples. Notably, the presence of 102−104 cells/mL of E. coli is mostly responsible for serious health issues [53].

To examine the selectivity of the CDs-MnO2 sensory platform towards E. coli, the same fluorimetric assay was carried out on other bacterial species of different classes (Gram-positive, Gram-negative, acid-fast). The fluorimetric studies with CDs-MnO2 platform demonstrated no change in fluorescence intensities of the aqueous solutions containing 5.0 × 108 CFU/mL representative bacteria of various classes like Gram-positive S. aureus, E. faecalis, Gram-negative V. cholera, P. aeruginosa and acid-fast M. smegmatis (Fig. 5 ). This excellent selectivity of the sensing platform is attributed to the redox enzyme mediated unique two-electron reduction of BQ to HQ, which is prevalent in E. coli, which distinguishes it from other bacterial strains.

Further, the specificity of CDs-MnO2 conjugate was investigated against some reductant biospecies like ascorbic acid (AA) [40] and GSH [38,39], considering some of them may be present in real samples or in the living system and they are also reported to respond to CDs-MnO2 conjugate. The fluorescence output of CDs from 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL of E. coli after incubation with increasing amounts of AA and GSH for 1 h followed by incubation with BQ solution showed a nominal change in fluorescence intensities up to 200 and 100 μM for AA and GSH, respectively (Fig. S9, SI). Thus, the CDs-MnO2 conjugate based sensing assay showed sufficient tolerance against reductant biospecies indicating its utility in the selective detection of E. coli. As expected, the small quantity of reductant (AA or GSH) can be converted to its oxidized form in the sensing assay as large excess of BQ (4 mM) is already present and also, there is a chance of bacteria eating-up these species. Therefore, the interference was seen only in the high concentrations of each of these species with increased fluorescence response for AA and GSH (Fig. S9, SI). However, even if present, the concentrations of these species in the bacterial samples are expected to be much lower than the tolerance level attributing no interference to the spontaneous detection of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic aspect of redox-coupled CDs-MnO2 platform for detecting E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing response is based on a series of redox reactions. It is known in the literature that compounds with redox behavior like BQ, ferricyanide, dichlorophenolindophenol are spontaneously reduced during the cell respiration of E. coli by the presence of oxidoreductases [41,54]. Presumably, the membrane-bound NADH-quinone reductase [55] is mainly responsible for a two-electron reduction of BQ to HQ under aerobic condition [56,57], the pathway which is more prevalent in E. coli; in contrary, several other bacterial species (such as S. aureus) [58] follow a single electron pathway to produce semiquinone as the major product. The hydroquinone formation is indicated by a purple-red colored complex formation between BQHQ [57]. Notably, Sun et al. previously used this phenomenon for the selective detection of E. coli by colorimetric and electrochemical method using BQ as a redox mediator [19]. For the present sensing process, a relay of redox reactions is rationally placed to eventually obtain a turn-on response from CDs upon interaction with E. coli. As mentioned earlier, CDs-MnO2 conjugate forms a FRET-pair in the working solution and remains in the turn-off state. Apparently, when E. coli are present in the system, the membrane-bound enzyme NADH-quinone reductase spontaneously reduces BQ to HQ by a two-electron transfer pathway [56,57]. The strong reductant, HQ, in turn, reacts with MnO2 nanosheets to quickly disintegrate it to discrete Mn2+ ions. Thus, the CDs are released into the aqueous solution and as they go to a disperse phase, their inherent blue fluorescence turns-on gradually (Scheme 1 ). As the two-electron reduction of BQ to HQ is more prevalent in E. coli, unlike other bacterial species, the present sensing system could selectively detect E. coli, whereas, other bacterial species remain unresponsive to the sensing assay. However, microbes possess several enzymes and the physiological function is quite complex. Thus, we presume that the selection of NADH-quinone reductase and two-electron reduction is the best possible way to explain the observed selectivity towards E. coli. However, further investigation is required to get a deep insight into biological function and activity to establish selectivity for E. coli. The preconceived mechanistic pathway was verified by a series of experiments. First, the release of CD nanoparticles, thereby providing a fluorescence response, from CDs-MnO2 conjugate was established by the gradual addition of HQ (0–160 μM). As seen in Fig. S10, SI, a linear plot was obtained with a saturation point close to 100 μM of HQ for the amount of CDs-MnO2 conjugate present in the given volume of the aqueous solution. This establishes the role of HQ (produced from BQ) in the redox relay process leading to the release of fluorescent CDs. The decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets from CDs-MnO2 conjugate was also established by UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing platform in the presence of variable concentrations of HQ (Fig. S11, SI). It was observed that with the increasing HQ concentration in the solution, the signature absorption band of MnO2 at 400 nm decreases slowly, indicating the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ (Fig. S11, SI). Further, the zeta potential and DLS values of the various steps of the events in the sensing process were captured to establish the proposed mechanism (Fig. S12). At first, CDs-MnO2 showed a total surface charge of -18.04 mV, and upon the addition of HQ solution, surface zeta potential decreased down to -12.86 mV. The decrease in surface charge is presumably because of the formation of positively charged Mn2+ in the solution (Fig. S1, SI). However, in the case of E. coli, only a slight decrease in the negatively charged surface (-15.38 mV) was observed. This is expected as the surface charge of Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli partly compensates the overall charge. Next, an abrupt reduction in the average size of CDs-MnO2 conjugate (from 224 nm to 20 nm) was seen upon the addition of HQ to the solution containing CDs-MnO2 conjugate indicating the disintegration of large MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. S12d). However, the sensing solution containing E. coli showed an average size of 812 nm (Fig. S12e), which is well-expected as much bigger bacterial entities are present in the solution. Finally, a sensing assay was tested with dead cells of E. coli in the aqueous solution. The fluorescence responses of CDs-MnO2 conjugate against the nutrient-deficient E. coli culture were recorded at regular intervals (0, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h, Fig. S13, SI). As expected, there was a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of sensing assay after 12 h, since cell respiration was gradually aborted due to the increasing number of dead cells in the solution. The fluorescence continued to decrease further till 48 h. After 72 h, there was no fluorescence response from the solution indicating no live bacterial colonies were present. Thus, the sensing assay is connected with cell respiration and works well for the detection of virulent live bacteria. Overall, the experimental results strongly support the proposed sensing process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E. coli in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the importance and applicability of on-site detection of E. coli, the present sensing assay was employed to real samples like rainwater, potato pulp and apple juice. All the real samples were directly spiked with known concentrations of E. coli, incubated for 1 h, and the corresponding fluorescence response was recorded (Fig. 6 ). In the case of rainwater, the recovery percentage was close to 100 % for each sample solution. For potato pulp, the recovery was a little low at a lower level of bacterial colonies (102 CFU/mL) (Table S3, SI). This is presumably because of the difficulty of capturing low fluorescence output from a little hazy solution. However, the study revealed the potential of the sensing platform in the identification and quantitation of E. coli in environmental and food samples.

Further, a separate study was conducted to detect the presence of E. coli in stagnant rainwater in the field. Therefore, the rainwater was artificially maintained on the soil. In two separate sets, one by seeding few bacterial colonies and another without that, the rainwater samples were aged, and the aliquots were taken after a certain time interval, filtered and incubated with BQ, CDs-MnO2 sensing solution was added, and corresponding fluorescence was recorded (Fig. S14, SI). A nominal growth was seen for the rainwater sample without seeding. However, the growth of the sample after the seeding of E. coli was significant after 24 h (Fig. S15, SI). After 72 h, however, not much growth was seen as it is expected that the bacterial growth in the absence of proper culture media is difficult and many cells will die with time. Once again, the study exhibits the potential applicability of the sensing assay for on-site detection of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Image analysis for the detection of E. coli in solution phase and on test-strips
ce:section.ce:para: With the sheer advancement in smartphone based devices, detection and quantitation of analytes by a quick analysis of images has become an important and user-friendly approach [59]. We felt, it will be worth carrying out image analysis for the quantitation of E. coli in the solution phase. The images of variable concentrations of bacterial colonies were captured by a 64 megapixels mobile camera and processed through image-J analysis software [50]. A good linear plot between corrected total cell fluorescence (CTCF) and concentration of E. coli with high regression coefficient (R2 = 0.9880) was obtained from 5 × 102 CFU/mL onwards (Fig. 7 a and b). The known concentrations of E. coli live colonies were plotted in the newly obtained standard calibration curve and cross-verified with the standard fluorimetric assay plots. The close match suggested that the smartphone based image analysis is also possible making a new avenue for on-site bacterial detection. Separately, as a demonstration study, visual detection of E. coli in test-strips under UV-light was carried out by probing the CDs-MnO2 conjugate in agar made polymer films and exposing them to the bacterial solution. For the preparation of test-strips, an aqueous solution of CDs-MnO2 was added to agar, and it was microwaved and dried to get a non-fluorescent film (the CDs in turn-off state by adhering to MnO2 surface). The film was cut into small pieces and those were dipped in the solution containing known concentrations of E. coli and BQ for 2 h, dried, placed under UV-light for visual change in fluorescence and thereafter, photographed using a mobile camera. As seen in Fig. 7c, the fluorescence from the test-strips became brighter with the exposure to higher concentrations of bacteria. More importantly, the solid phase provides sufficient fluorescence response from a 1.0 × 102 CFU/mL bacterial solution, which is usually the number of colonies present in the stale food. Further, the CTCF vs. CFU/mL plot showed linearity with a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9819 (Fig. 7d). It is presumed that the higher bacterial concentration produces more HQs in the solution which slowly penetrate into the solid surface of the agar-polymer based test-strips through small pores, react with MnO2 nanosheets in the way and break apart loose CDs. The CDs show bright blue fluorescence while still adhering onto the solid surface. At present, the development of a mobile app-based fluorimetric tool using this technology is going on in our laboratory.

Finally, the current redox coupled CDs-MnO2 based novel sensing assay was compared with available CDs-based sensors (Table 1 ) and other sensing tools for E. coli (Table S4). Clearly, the present method has stand-out advantages over the existing ones in terms of easy and cost-effective synthesis from biomass, selectivity towards E. coli, tolerance to certain bio-reductants, high sensitivity, low LOD, etc. Unlike many other reported methods, the present sensing assay detects only fetal live bacteria and does not count the dead bacteria, as it is based on cell-respiration and not electrostatic or site-specific interactions. Moreover, smartphone based image capturing and analysis, and fluorescent detection on test-strips was first time demonstrated for E. coli, which are advantageous for on-site detection purposes.

In summary, a novel redox coupled carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheets FRET-conjugate based fluorescent sensing platform has been devised for sensitive, selective and rapid detection of Escherichia coliin aqueous media. A unique enzymatic redox relay reaction, mediated by a redox couple of p-benzoquinone and hydroquinone, was exploited in the detection process. Presumably, the CDs-MnO2 FRET pair, at turn-off state, responds to E. coli by turning-on the inherent blue fluorescence of CDs by an enzyme mediated redox reaction. The enzyme, presumably NADH-quinone reductases first converts BQ to HQ, which in turn reacts with MnO2 nanosheets to quickly disintegrate it to discrete Mn2+ ions. Consequently, the CDs are released into the aqueous solution to trigger a turn on fluorescence. The reported detection tool holds a linear relationship with E. coli colonies in a wide range of concentrations between 102-109 CFU/mL. The LOD was determined as 50 CFU/mL for E. coli. Further, the utility of this cost-effective sensing tool was demonstrated in real-sample analysis, and quantitation of E. coli was done in rainwater and food samples. Moreover, the detection of E. coli was demonstrated by image analysis of the solution phase and on agar polymer based test-strips as a demonstration of on-site detection of E. coli. The CDs-MnO2 based sensing platform with high selectivity and sensitivity, fast response and low detection limit is a potential alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of E. coli.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sharanabasava D. Hiremath is pursuing his doctoral degree under the supervision of Dr. Amrita Chatterjee Department of Chemistry, BITS Pilani K. K. Birla Goa Campus, Goa. His research areas include nanomaterial based sensors AIE-active sensors and their applications.

Akhil A. Bhosle is pursuing his doctoral degree under the supervision of Dr. Mainak Banerjee Department of Chemistry, BITS Pilani K. K. Birla Goa Campus, Goa. He works in the areas of green synthesis of dyes and supramolecular sensors.
Anushka Nayse completed her B Tech degree from BITS Pilani KK Birla Goa Campus. She is interested in image analysis and app development.

Dr. Sumit Biswas did his Masters in Biophysics and Molecular Biology in 2002 from the University of Calcutta, and a PhD in Biochemistry and Structural Biology (2008) from Bose Institute. After a postdoctoral fellowship as DBT-RA, he joined the Department of Biological Sciences at BITS Pilani KK Birla Goa campus. Sumit Biswas' group has been working on the molecular biology of pathogens and examining the role of proteins in biofilm formation for the last 12 years. He also specializes in biophysical and structural characterization of macromolecules.

Dr. Malabika Biswas obtained a PhD in Biochemistry and Molecular biology in 2008 from Bose Institute, Kolkata. Thereafter she joined the Department of Biological sciences at BITS Pilani, K.K.Birla Goa campus (India). She has been doing extensive research on the molecular biology of bacteriophage Φ11, a S. aureus temperate phage. The gene regulatory elements of Φ11 can be studied and assembled to construct tightly regulated vectors. Dr. Biswas is also working on combatting multi-drug resistant pathogens using phage therapy.

Dr. Achikanath C. Bhasikuttan is a Scientific Officer in Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, India, obtained his Ph.D. from the University of Mumbai in 1998 and completed his post doctoral research as a JSPS postdoctoral fellow at Osaka University, Japan during 1999−2001. His research interests include the excited state molecular dynamics, time resolved spectroscopy, energy/electron transfer interactions and to probe the intricacies of non-covalent interactions in supra-biomolecular systems.

Mainak Banerjee received his PhD in chemistry from Jadavpur University, India in 2006. Presently, he is an associate professor of chemistry at BITS Pilani KK Birla Goa Campus, India. His current research interests are green chemistry, supramolecular chemistry and sensor.

Amrita Chatterjee received her PhD degree in chemistry from Jadavpur University, India in 2006. Presently she is an associate professor of chemistry in BITS Pilani KK Birla Goa Campus. Her current research interests include development of molecular sensor using AIE based molecule, different materials based sensors and green chemistry.
